title: Chapter 17: The Voicemail
content: Chloe got three voicemails on her first full day of freedom.
The first was from a reporter. She deleted it.
The second was from her mother. She stared at the screen for thirty seconds, then deleted that too.
The third was from Jake.
She did not delete that one. She listened to it three times.
His voice was exactly how she remembered it. Warm. Earnest. The voice of a man who had never been told no and had never learned how to handle it.

*"Hey, Chloe. It's me. Look — I know things ended badly between us. But I heard about what happened at that place, and I just want you to know I had nothing to do with it. Your dad called me, asked me to sign some papers, said it was for your own good. I didn't know what it was. I swear."*
A pause.
*"I'd really like to talk. Just to clear things up. Call me back when you get this."*
Chloe set the phone down on the kitchen counter.

She had imagined this moment for weeks. In the quiet hours of Isolation, she had rehearsed what she would say to him. She had written three different versions of a speech. She had imagined his face when she finally confronted him with the truth.
Now the call was here, and she felt nothing.
Not anger. Not satisfaction. Not even the cold satisfaction she had expected.
Just... tired.
Elena walked into the kitchen and saw her staring at the phone. "Bad news?"
"Jake."
"Your ex-boyfriend?"
"The one who signed the commitment papers."
Elena's expression hardened. "What did he want?"
"To explain. To apologize. To pretend he was a victim of my father's manipulation."
"Are you going to call him back?"
Chloe picked up the phone. Turned it over in her hands. Set it back down.
"Not yet."
"That's smart. Let him wait."
"It's not strategy. I just don't have the energy to deal with him right now."
Elena studied her for a moment. "That's not a bad thing. It means you're healing."
"Is that what this is? Healing?"
"The first stage. The stage where you stop letting them live in your head."
Chloe looked at the phone. The screen had gone dark. Jake's voice was still echoing in her ears, but it was fading. Like a song she had heard too many times.
"I want to go back," she said.
"Back where?"
"To the facility. I want to see it now that it's empty."
Elena drove her.

Serenity Peak looked different in the afternoon light. The news vans were gone. The police tape was gone. The only cars in the lot belonged to the county transport team, processing the last of the patient releases.
Chloe walked through the front doors without anyone stopping her.

The lobby was empty. The front desk was abandoned, papers scattered across the counter. A coffee cup sat half-drunk on the receptionist's desk, as if someone had walked away mid-sip and never come back.
She walked through the hallways, past the common room, past the admin wing. Every door was open. Every room was empty. The facility that had held her for thirty-two days was already decaying.
She stopped at the door of her old room.
Bed stripped. Sheets gone. The window still had the scratch marks from where she had pried at the seal with her fingernails during the first week.
*The first week,* she thought. *When I was still trying to escape. Before I realized I had to burn the whole place down instead.*
She turned and walked away.
At the end of the hall, a door was closed.
The admin office.
She pushed it open.
Dr. Webber was sitting behind his desk.

He looked up when she entered. His suit was still pressed. His hair was still combed. But his eyes were different. The calm arrogance was gone. In its place was something that looked almost like defeat.
"Ms. Hart."
"Dr. Webber."
They looked at each other across the desk. The same desk where she had sat during intake, where he had explained the rules of the facility in that smooth, professional voice.
"I'm surprised you're still here," she said.
"I'm cleaning out my office." He gestured to a cardboard box on the floor. Personal items. A framed diploma. A photo of his family.
"They fired you."
"Suspended pending investigation. The board will make a final decision next week."
"You know what they're going to decide."
He did not answer.
Chloe stepped into the room. She did not sit down. She stood in front of his desk, looking down at him for the first time.
"Do you remember what you told me on my first day?" she asked.
"I told you many things."
"You told me that this place was designed to help me. That I would learn to be a better version of myself. That I would thank you someday."
He was quiet.
"I'm not going to thank you," she said. "I'm going to make sure everyone knows exactly what you did here."
"The media already knows."
"The media knows the headline. They don't know the details. The women who were given drugs they didn't need. The families who were manipulated. The records that were falsified."
She pulled out her phone.
"I have recordings. I have documents. I have testimonies from every woman who walked out of here. And I'm going to make sure every single one of them is heard."
Webber's face went pale.
"Why?" he asked. "What do you get out of this?"
Chloe looked at him. The man who had built an empire on the misery of women who were inconvenient to the people who were supposed to love them.
"Satisfaction," she said.
She turned and walked out.
Elena was waiting by the car. She saw Chloe's face and did not ask. She just opened the passenger door.
Chloe got in. The seat was warm from the sun. She leaned back and closed her eyes.
"He's broken," she said.
"Good."
"I thought it would feel better."
"It never does right away. The satisfaction comes later, when you're living your life and you realize you never have to think about him again."
Chloe opened her eyes and looked at the facility through the windshield. The building that had been her prison for thirty-two days. The building that had tried to break her and failed.
"I want to buy it," she said.
Elena turned to look at her. "What?"
"The facility. It's going to be sold. The parent company is liquidating. Someone is going to buy it and turn it into something else."
"And you want to be that someone?"
"Not me. Us. Tiffany, Amara, Mia, me. We turn it into what it was supposed to be — a real place for women who need help. Not a prison. Not a conversion camp. An actual sanctuary."
Elena was quiet for a long moment.
"That's going to take money," she said.
"I know."
"A lot of money."
"I know."
"And a legal structure. And a board. And — "
"I know all of that," Chloe said. "But I also know that we just took down a multinational wellness conglomerate with a burner phone and a manifesto. I think we can figure out the paperwork."
Elena stared at her. Then she laughed.
"You know what?" she said. "I think you can."
